title: Why Новый год, not Christmas, is Russia's biggest holiday
bodyMd:
In most Christian countries, Christmas is the year's biggest family holiday. In Russia, it's Новый год (New Year) — and the reason is Soviet history, not accident.

After the 1917 revolution, the officially atheist Soviet state suppressed religious holiday celebration, and Christmas (along with its tree, carols, and gift-giving) was banned outright in the late 1920s. In 1935, the government revived the decorated fir tree and a gift-giving figure — but rebranded them as secular fixtures of Новый год instead, stripped of religious meaning, so people could keep the traditions they loved without a state-forbidden religious holiday attached to them. Дед Мороз, a figure descended from older Slavic winter/frost folklore, was recast as this secular gift-giver.

That's why, decades after the Soviet Union's collapse, Новый год remains the anchor celebration — with a multi-day public holiday, family gatherings, gifts, and the midnight countdown — while Orthodox Christmas (7 January, on the Church's Julian calendar) is observed mainly as a quieter religious occasion, not the commercial and emotional center of the season.

title: Дед Мороз is not "Russian Santa Claus"
bodyMd:
It's tempting to translate Дед Мороз as "Russian Santa Claus," but the two figures differ in nearly every concrete detail beyond "old man who brings gifts."

- **Entrance**: Santa comes down the chimney while everyone sleeps. Дед Мороз walks in through the front door, in person, usually while the family is awake and can talk with him directly — many Russian households have young children literally recite a poem or sing a song to him before he hands over presents.
- **Companion**: Дед Мороз is never alone — he's always accompanied by his granddaughter Снегурочка (the Snow Maiden), a character with no equivalent anywhere in the Santa Claus tradition.
- **Timing**: gifts arrive on the night of 31 December, tied to Новый год, not Christmas Eve.
- **Look**: a long blue or red fur-trimmed шуба (coat) reaching to the ankles, а wooden or crystal посох (staff), and валенки (felt boots) — not a short red-and-white suit and black boots.
- **Transport and home base**: no flying reindeer sleigh — traditionally a horse-drawn тройка (three-horse sleigh). Since the 1990s he has an official "residence" in the town of Velikiy Ustyug, not the North Pole.

The resemblance is real (both are white-bearded winter gift-givers), but the lore, iconography, and ritual around Дед Мороз developed on its own track.

title: The smell of мандарины means New Year to every Russian
bodyMd:
Ask almost any Russian what New Year smells like, and мандарины (tangerines) will come up before anything else — including the ёлка itself.

The association goes back to Soviet times, when citrus fruit was a genuine scarcity for most of the year. Tangerines shipped in from Abkhazia and, later, Morocco and Cuba, became available in stores almost exclusively in the weeks around New Year, making them a small but eagerly anticipated luxury rather than an everyday snack. Crates of them showing up in shops was itself an informal seasonal signal that Новый год was near.

Mandarins are cheap and available year-round in Russia today, but the Proustian link never faded: the smell of peeling one still triggers New Year childhood memories for people who grew up decades after the scarcity that created the association in the first place.

title: Салат Оливье: from imperial luxury to Soviet-era staple
bodyMd:
The dish appears on nearly every Russian New Year table, but it started as something almost nobody today would recognize.

It was invented in the 1860s by Lucien Olivier, a Belgian-born chef running a fashionable Moscow restaurant, and the original recipe was a status dish for the wealthy: grouse, veal tongue, caviar, crayfish tails, and capers in a house-made mayonnaise-style sauce whose exact recipe Olivier reportedly never wrote down.

The Soviet-era version that survives today is a practical reinvention born of shortage and rationing rather than a simplification for taste: grouse and crayfish became diced potato, carrot, boiled egg, canned peas, pickles, and докторская колбаса (a cheap boiled sausage/bologna), all bound in shop-bought mayonnaise. The result was inexpensive, filling, and endlessly stretchable for a crowd — exactly what made it spread to virtually every household regardless of region or income, and why it's now treated as close to mandatory rather than optional on 31 December.

title: The paper-wish-in-champagne ritual
bodyMd:
Beyond simply making a wish as the бой курантов strikes, many Russians — especially younger and urban ones — practice a specific, more elaborate version of the custom, half-joking and half-serious at the same time.

During the twelve strikes of the Kremlin chimes at midnight, you write your wish on a small strip of paper, set it alight (over a candle or in an ashtray), and let it burn down to ash. The ash then goes into a бокал of шампанское — stirred in or simply dropped — which you must drink before the twelfth and final chime finishes sounding, roughly a 36-second window in total.

The custom has no religious or official backing; it's closer to a shared, semi-superstitious game everyone privately half-believes works. Successfully finishing the wish, the burn, and the drink before the chimes stop is treated as good luck for getting what you wished for in the coming year — timing it is part of the fun, and fumbling it (a badly lit match, a slow pour) is a running joke at many New Year's gatherings.

title: «Ирония судьбы, или С лёгким паром!» — the film all of Russia watches
bodyMd:
Eldar Ryazanov's 1975 comedy «Ирония судьбы, или С лёгким паром!» ("The Irony of Fate, or Enjoy Your Bath!") is arguably the single biggest shared cultural touchstone of the Russian New Year.

The plot: on 31 December, a Moscow doctor goes to the banya (steam bath) with friends as an annual tradition, gets too drunk to notice he's been mistakenly put on a flight to Leningrad instead of staying in Moscow, and — because Soviet mass housing built identical apartment blocks with identical street names and identical-looking buildings across cities — takes a taxi to "his own" address, lets himself in with his own key (it fits), and falls asleep in what he assumes is his apartment. It belongs to a stranger, and the ensuing New Year's Eve encounter becomes the film's romantic core.

Channel One has aired it during New Year's programming nearly every year since its release — so consistently that quoting a line from it is assumed common knowledge the way Russians assume everyone recognizes the phrase С лёгким паром itself. Watching it, at least partially, in the background of New Year's Eve or Day is less a deliberate choice for many families than simply what's on.

title: Старый Новый год — a charming second New Year on 13 January
bodyMd:
Russia effectively gets two New Year's celebrations, thanks to a 13-day mismatch between calendars.

The Russian Orthodox Church still uses the older Julian calendar for its religious calendar, which is why Orthodox Christmas falls on 7 January rather than 25 December. The same 13-day offset means that the pre-revolutionary Julian New Year's Eve lands on the night of 13-14 January in the modern Gregorian calendar — a date now nicknamed Старый Новый год, "Old New Year."

It isn't an official public holiday and nobody gets the day off work, but it's still commonly marked with a smaller repeat of New Year's traditions: another toast, sometimes another modest home-cooked spread, often just among close family or friends who didn't want the season to end quite yet. It functions as an informal, low-key coda to the roughly two-week holiday stretch that runs from 31 December through it — proof that in Russia, one New Year's Eve is apparently not always enough.
